findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a large, discrete, shallow ulceration—often termed a "giant" ulcer—located within the mid to distal esophagus on a barium swallow [1]. The floor of the ulcer appears clean and smooth, typically bordered by a well-defined lucent rim representing edema [1].
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include an otherwise normal-appearing background esophageal mucosa without diffuse nodularity or rigidity [2]. This presentation typically correlates with a clinical history of severe odynophagia in an immunocompromised patient, such as one with acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS) [3].
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of a diffuse 'shaggy' contour with plaque-like filling defects, which would suggest Candida esophagitis [4, 5]. Furthermore, there are no multiple, tiny, discrete ulcers to suggest Herpes Simplex Virus (HSV) esophagitis [2, 6].
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of a solitary, large, discrete focal ulcer against a background of relatively normal mucosa [2].

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Viral esophagitis (CMV), given the presence of a discrete, giant ulcer in the mid-to-distal esophagus [1, 3]. This is a classic radiological presentation for CMV or HIV esophagitis in profoundly immunosuppressed patients [2].
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Reﬂ ux esophagitis, however this condition typically demonstrates thickened distal longitudinal folds, linear ulcerations extending upwards from the gastroesophageal junction, and commonly an associated sliding hiatal hernia [7, 8]. The discrete, isolated nature of the giant ulcer seen here without severe diffuse distal inflammatory changes makes pure reflux less likely.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Drug-induced esophagitis, given that focal contact injury from prolonged retention of medications can identically present as a solitary large ulcer [6]. Missing this could lead to persistent mucosal injury if the offending oral medication is not discontinued or properly managed.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to characterize the ulcer's size and location while advising endoscopic correlation, as differentiating between CMV and HIV ulceration radiologically is impossible [3].
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Reviewing previous barium studies could establish chronicity or document pre-existing strictures or motility disorders that might predispose to pill retention.
management.intervention: If tissue diagnosis is needed, an endoscopic approach is the optimal target, obtaining brushings or biopsy specimens from the base of the ulcer to detect characteristic intranuclear inclusion bodies and initiate targeted antiviral agents such as ganciclovir [3].
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Gastroenterology and Infectious Diseases MDT. My report would clearly document the exact location, size, and depth of the ulcer, alongside the absence of malignant features such as shouldering or an irregular mass, to expedite definitive endoscopic assessment and appropriate medical therapy.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a large, discrete, shallow ulceration—often termed a "giant" ulcer—located within the mid to distal esophagus on a barium swallow [1]. The floor of the ulcer appears clean and smooth, typically bordered by a well-defined lucent rim representing edema [1]. Supporting features include an otherwise normal-appearing background esophageal mucosa without diffuse nodularity or rigidity [2]. This presentation typically correlates with a clinical history of severe odynophagia in an immunocompromised patient, such as one with acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS) [3]. Importantly, there is no evidence of a diffuse 'shaggy' contour with plaque-like filling defects, which would suggest Candida esophagitis [4, 5]. Furthermore, there are no multiple, tiny, discrete ulcers to suggest Herpes Simplex Virus (HSV) esophagitis [2, 6]. The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of a solitary, large, discrete focal ulcer against a background of relatively normal mucosa [2].

The appearances are most in keeping with Viral esophagitis (CMV), given the presence of a discrete, giant ulcer in the mid-to-distal esophagus [1, 3]. This is a classic radiological presentation for CMV or HIV esophagitis in profoundly immunosuppressed patients [2]. The principle differential would be Reﬂ ux esophagitis, however this condition typically demonstrates thickened distal longitudinal folds, linear ulcerations extending upwards from the gastroesophageal junction, and commonly an associated sliding hiatal hernia [7, 8]. The discrete, isolated nature of the giant ulcer seen here without severe diffuse distal inflammatory changes makes pure reflux less likely. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Drug-induced esophagitis, given that focal contact injury from prolonged retention of medications can identically present as a solitary large ulcer [6]. Missing this could lead to persistent mucosal injury if the offending oral medication is not discontinued or properly managed.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to characterize the ulcer's size and location while advising endoscopic correlation, as differentiating between CMV and HIV ulceration radiologically is impossible [3]. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Reviewing previous barium studies could establish chronicity or document pre-existing strictures or motility disorders that might predispose to pill retention. If tissue diagnosis is needed, an endoscopic approach is the optimal target, obtaining brushings or biopsy specimens from the base of the ulcer to detect characteristic intranuclear inclusion bodies and initiate targeted antiviral agents such as ganciclovir [3]. This should go to the Gastroenterology and Infectious Diseases MDT. My report would clearly document the exact location, size, and depth of the ulcer, alongside the absence of malignant features such as shouldering or an irregular mass, to expedite definitive endoscopic assessment and appropriate medical therapy.
